title: Symposium

cover.description: Cover of 2003 Penguin Classics paperback edition.
cover.alt: Book cover showing part of the side of an ancient Greek vase showing men reclining and standing at a banquet raising drinking vessels. Text underneath the illustration reads 'Penguin Classics / PLATO / The Symposium'

overview: Plato's philosophical dialogue about the nature of love, sex, friendship and desire has been read and studied for millennia. Featuring several speakers at a banquet involving much drinking, the topics it touches on include same-sex relations (including of men with boys), an origin story of desire effectively involving three genders, and sources of happiness ranging from earthy pleasure to the appreciation of beauty and truth.

incidents.description: When the Texas A&M board of Regents adopted a {ln:https://policies.tamus.edu/08-01.pdf|policy} prohibiting core courses from 'advocat[ing] race or gender ideology, or topics related to sexual orientation or gender identity', professor Martin Peterson tweaked the {ln:https://dailynous.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/peterson-martin-syllabus-phil-111-s-2026-texas.pdf|syllabus} of his introductory philosophy class to include a 'Race and Gender Ideology' section featuring readings from Plato's Symposium. Once informed of this, university administrators {ln:https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/culture-features/plato-texas-am-university-censorship-philosophy-1235497060/|ordered} Peterson to remove the 'modules on race and gender ideology, and the Plato readings that may include these', or be removed from teaching the course. The university's move was met with condemnation from many academics and advocacy groups, including FIRE, who {ln:https://www.fire.org/news/texas-am-philosophy-professor-nix-plato-or-be-reassigned|responded} with a letter to Texas A&M administration and a statement that 'You don’t protect students by banning 2,400-year-old philosophy.'
